thinking-in-objects.label: Thinking in Objects
thinking-in-objects.summary: Use provided Account objects first so object creation, attributes, and method calls make sense before you write class syntax.

sketch0.title: Create Account objects to represent real things

sketch1.title: Read values stored on an object

sketch2.title: Ask one object to do something

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch0.title: Create two provided Account objects
try_thinking_in_objects_sketch0.prompt: An `Account` model is already provided in `models/account.py`. Do not edit that file yet. In `main.py`, create two objects: `Account("Nora", 200)` and `Account("Eli", 45)`. Store each object in its own variable. Do not print anything in this first exercise.
try_thinking_in_objects_sketch0.hint: Use the provided class exactly as-is and focus only on object creation.

help.concept: At this stage, one object stands for one account in the program. You are practicing how to create objects before worrying about how the class is written.
help.hint_1: Create one variable for `Account("Nora", 200)` and another for `Account("Eli", 45)`.
help.hint_2: This exercise stops after object creation, so printing is not required.

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch0.starterCode:
from models.account import Account

# A working Account class is already provided.
# Create two Account objects below.
# Do not print anything yet.

main_py.content:
from models.account import Account

# A working Account class is already provided.
# Create two Account objects below.
# Do not print anything yet.

from models.account import Account

rainy_day = Account("Nora", 200)
snack_fund = Account("Eli", 45)

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch0.solutionCode:
from models.account import Account

rainy_day = Account("Nora", 200)
snack_fund = Account("Eli", 45)

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch1.title: Read attributes from two Account objects
try_thinking_in_objects_sketch1.prompt: Keep using the provided `Account` model. In `main.py`, create `Account("Jae", 55)` and `Account("Priya", 210)`. Then print the first account owner on one line and the second account balance on the next line.
try_thinking_in_objects_sketch1.hint: Use dot notation after each object is created.

help.concept: An attribute is a value stored on one object. Dot notation like `account.owner` reads that stored value.
help.hint_1: Create both objects first so each variable points at one account.
help.hint_2: Then print `travel_fund.owner` and `rent_fund.balance` on separate lines.

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch1.starterCode:
from models.account import Account

# Create two Account objects below.
# Then print one attribute from each object.

main_py.content:
from models.account import Account

# Create two Account objects below.
# Then print one attribute from each object.

from models.account import Account

travel_fund = Account("Jae", 55)
rent_fund = Account("Priya", 210)

print(travel_fund.owner)
print(rent_fund.balance)

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch1.solutionCode:
from models.account import Account

travel_fund = Account("Jae", 55)
rent_fund = Account("Priya", 210)

print(travel_fund.owner)
print(rent_fund.balance)

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch2.title: Call a method on one provided Account object
try_thinking_in_objects_sketch2.prompt: Use the provided `Account` model again. In `main.py`, create `Account("Omar", 70)`, call `deposit(15)` on that object, and then print `summary()`. Do not edit `models/account.py` yet.
try_thinking_in_objects_sketch2.hint: Create the object, call the method on that same object, then print the returned summary.

help.concept: A method is behavior that belongs to one object. Here, `deposit` changes that object's balance and `summary` reads the updated state.
help.hint_2: Then print `account.summary()` so the changed balance appears.

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch2.starterCode:
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Omar", 70)

# Call deposit on this object.
# Then print account.summary().

main_py.content:
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Omar", 70)

# Call deposit on this object.
# Then print account.summary().

from models.account import Account

account = Account("Omar", 70)
account.deposit(15)

print(account.summary())

try_thinking_in_objects_sketch2.solutionCode:
from models.account import Account

account = Account("Omar", 70)
account.deposit(15)

print(account.summary())

defines_class.message: Keep using the provided Account class.

method_sequence_returns.message: After the deposit, summary() should show the updated balance.

mc-thinking-in-objects-object-idea.title: What makes an object useful?
mc-thinking-in-objects-object-idea.prompt: Which ideas match the beginner view of objects from this topic? Choose all that apply.
mc-thinking-in-objects-object-idea.hint: Think about keeping related data and actions together around one program thing.

help.concept: In this topic, an object stands for one thing the program cares about and keeps that thing's related data and behavior together.
help.hint_1: Pick the answers about grouping values and actions around one account.
help.hint_2: Reject answers that turn objects into random unrelated variables.

options.a: An object can represent one account in the program.
options.b: An object can keep related values and actions together.
options.c: An object only matters after you memorize class syntax.
options.d: An object means every account must share the exact same balance.

sc-thinking-in-objects-attribute-meaning.title: What is an attribute here?
sc-thinking-in-objects-attribute-meaning.prompt: In this topic, what is an attribute like `account.owner`?
sc-thinking-in-objects-attribute-meaning.hint: It is a value stored on one specific object.

help.concept: An attribute is stored data that belongs to one object.
help.hint_1: Think about the owner's name living on one account object.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about a stored value, not a separate program file.

options.a: A value stored on one object
options.b: A file that imports the class
options.c: A Python keyword for making loops

fb-thinking-in-objects-method-call.title: Complete the method call
fb-thinking-in-objects-method-call.prompt: Fill in the missing method name so the object adds money to its own balance.
fb-thinking-in-objects-method-call.hint: Use the method from the topic that changes the balance.

help.concept: A method call asks one object to do something with its own state.
help.hint_1: The object name is already there. The blank is only the method name.
help.hint_2: Choose the method that adds money, not the method that only returns text.

dr-thinking-in-objects-workflow.title: Order the object workflow
dr-thinking-in-objects-workflow.prompt: Put these beginner steps in order.
dr-thinking-in-objects-workflow.hint: Create the object before reading from it or calling its methods.

help.concept: A beginner object workflow is: create the object, read values if needed, call a method when you want a change, then use the result.
help.hint_1: The object must exist before any attribute read or method call can happen.
help.hint_2: Printing the result comes after the object has done the work.

tokens.t2: Read one attribute from that object
tokens.t3: Call one method on that object
tokens.t4: Print the result or updated summary

sketch-0.title: Create Account objects to represent real things
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
Before writing class syntax, it helps to understand the problem objects solve.

Imagine tracking two accounts with separate variables:

```python
first_owner = "Ava"
first_balance = 125
second_owner = "Ben"
second_balance = 80
```

That works for a tiny example, but the values that belong to one account are spread apart.

An object lets the program treat one account as one thing:

```python
checking = Account("Ava", 125)
savings = Account("Ben", 80)
```

You do not need to know how `Account` is built yet. For now, notice the idea:

- `checking` is one account object.
- `savings` is another account object.
- Each object starts with its own owner and balance.

sketch-1.title: Read values stored on an object
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
After an object exists, you can read the values stored on it.

Those stored values are called **attributes**.

```python
account = Account("Sofia", 90)
print(account.owner)
print(account.balance)
```

Dot notation means:

- `account.owner` reads the owner stored on that object.
- `account.balance` reads the balance stored on that object.

Different objects can keep different values:

```python
first = Account("Sofia", 90)
second = Account("Marco", 140)
print(first.balance)
print(second.balance)
```

sketch-2.title: Ask one object to do something
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
Objects can also have **methods**, which are actions you ask one object to perform.

```python
account = Account("Lina", 60)
account.deposit(20)
print(account.summary())
```

Here is the flow:

- `deposit(20)` changes that account's stored balance.
- `summary()` returns a readable description of the current state.

The big beginner idea is this: one object keeps its own data and can act on that same data.
